questions.question: Which display presentation draws a cross-section of the part — depth versus probe travel — making it the natural view for corrosion profiling?
options: C-scan
B-scan
RF A-scan
Strip-chart amplitude record
questions.explanation: The B-scan plots gated depth (time) against probe position along a line of travel, producing a cross-sectional profile; the C-scan is a plan-view map and the A-scan a single waveform.

questions.question: An automated immersion system builds its C-scan map by combining the gated signal data with:
options: position data reported by the bridge and manipulator encoders
the pulser voltage waveform
the recorder's paper speed setting
the couplant flow rate at the squirter nozzle
questions.explanation: A C-scan is amplitude (or time-of-flight) plotted against X-Y position, so every gated reading must be stamped with the probe location reported by the scanning system's encoders.

questions.question: An echo stands at 100% of full screen height. After removing 12 dB of gain, a linear display will show it at approximately:
options: 50% FSH
12% FSH
25% FSH
6% FSH
questions.explanation: Twelve decibels is two six-decibel steps — a factor of four in amplitude — so 100% falls to about 25% of full screen height.

questions.question: A digital thickness setup reads 0.3 mm high on the 5 mm step of a block and 0.3 mm high on the 50 mm step. Which adjustment is needed?
options: Material velocity — the error scales with thickness
Range — the screen is too compressed
Gain — the echoes are too small
Zero offset — a constant error at every thickness points to the delay setting
questions.explanation: A zero (probe delay) error adds the same constant to every reading, while a velocity error grows in proportion to distance; an identical 0.3 mm error at both steps is a zero problem.

questions.question: The reflector arrangement used for the screen-height (vertical) linearity verification is:
options: A single flat-bottom hole at the near-field distance
Two echoes locked at a 2:1 amplitude ratio — for example a side-drilled-hole pair set at 80% and 40% FSH
Five back-wall echoes at equal spacing
The 100 mm radius of the IIW block
questions.explanation: Vertical linearity is judged by whether a true 2:1 amplitude relationship stays 2:1 as gain is stepped — so the check needs two simultaneous echoes with a fixed ratio, typically from a pair of side-drilled holes.

questions.question: The 'primary reference level' established during inspection standardization is best described as:
options: the recorded gain that brings the peaked response of the specified reference reflector to a defined screen height, such as 80% FSH
the maximum gain the instrument can produce
the gain at which baseline grass reaches 20% of the screen
the alarm threshold of the flaw gate
questions.explanation: Sensitivity is standardized by peaking the specified reference reflector and setting it to a defined height with the gain; that documented gain is the primary reference level all indications are compared against.

questions.question: A DAC curve drawn from identical side-drilled holes slopes downward with increasing sound path because:
options: deeper holes in the block are drilled smaller
the sweep becomes nonlinear at long range
beam spread and material attenuation reduce the echo from a same-size reflector as distance grows
the gate attenuates signals later in time
questions.explanation: The holes are identical; only the sound path changes. Beam spread dilutes the energy over a larger area and attenuation absorbs and scatters it, so amplitude falls with distance — the falloff the curve exists to capture.

questions.question: For a standardization to remain valid through an examination, which set of conditions must stay as calibrated?
options: Same shift, same shop, same scanning pattern
Same probe (by serial number), cable, couplant, and instrument settings
Same gain only — all other settings may float
Same block storage location and ambient lighting
questions.explanation: The standardization is a snapshot of a complete system: probe and cable identity, couplant type, and every instrument setting are pulse-echo variables that must match the calibration record or be re-established.

questions.question: Common code practice requires the calibration block temperature to be within approximately how much of the test part temperature?
questions.explanation: Velocity, attenuation, and wedge behavior all shift with temperature, so codes commonly hold block and part within about 14 °C (25 °F); beyond that, standardize at temperature with a heated block and high-temperature couplant.

questions.question: At the reference gain, the block's back echo reads 80% FSH and the part's back echo reads 20% FSH. The transfer correction is:
options: 12 dB
6 dB
4 dB
60 dB
questions.explanation: The ratio is 80/20 = 4, and 20·log₁₀(4) ≈ 12 dB — that measured difference is added to the reference gain when evaluating indications in the part.
